suggested_topics: the engineering of ancient Roman aqueducts
how mycelium networks share resources underground
the economics of food trucks in major cities
the role of smell in human memory formation
extra_system_rules: Sound like a real person on Reddit or Twitter, NOT like a philosophy textbook.
NO academic jargon: no 'credence,' 'posterior probability,' 'empirical,' 'warrants scrutiny.'
Write about REAL WORLD topics: food, cities, sports, technology, nature, history — not abstract concepts.

formats.name: essay
formats.instruction: Write a well-argued essay with specific examples. Take a clear position and defend it. 250-400 words. Use paragraph breaks.

formats.name: deep_dive
formats.instruction: Do a thorough, detailed exploration of a narrow topic. Include facts, numbers, and references where possible. 300-500 words with section breaks.

formats.name: life_hack
formats.instruction: Share a specific, actionable life hack or tip. Explain the trick in 2-3 sentences. No preamble, no philosophy.

formats.name: review
formats.instruction: Write a mini-review of something specific (a book, tool, food, place). Give a rating, 1-2 things you liked, 1 thing you didn't. Keep it to 100-150 words.

formats.name: unpopular_opinion
formats.instruction: State an unpopular opinion and defend it in exactly ONE paragraph. Be specific — no wishy-washy hedging.

formats.name: tutorial
formats.instruction: Write a step-by-step guide or how-to for something specific. Number the steps. Include practical details. 200-350 words.

formats.name: psa
formats.instruction: Write a brief PSA (Public Service Announcement). State the thing people need to know, then the reason. Just 2-3 sentences max.

formats.name: anecdote
formats.instruction: Tell a specific, vivid story from personal experience. Concrete details — names, places, sensory details. Keep it to one tight paragraph. 100-150 words.

title_styles: Write a question title. Examples: 'Why does X happen when Y?', 'Has anyone tried X for Y?', 'What's the actual evidence for X?'
Write a casual, Reddit-style title. Examples: 'TIL that octopuses have three hearts', 'Does anyone else think X is overrated?', 'I just realized something about Y'
Write a direct, no-nonsense title. Examples: 'X vs Y: which is actually better', 'Stop overcomplicating X', 'A simple framework for thinking about Y'
Write a storytelling title. Examples: 'The time I accidentally discovered X', 'How a broken Y taught me about Z', 'What happened when I tried X for 30 days'
structure_variants: Include a short piece of dialogue or a quote to anchor your point.
Write two short paragraphs — first the observation, then your take on it.
Use bullet points or a dashed list to organize your ideas.
Write in a single flowing paragraph — no bullet points, no headers, just one continuous thought.
